abstract:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable success in single-label image classification. Translating this success to multi-label scenarios remains a challenge, however, due to the intricate spatial layouts of multiple objects and the relative scarcity of annotated multi-label training data. In this paper, we introduce Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP), a flexible deep learning infrastructure designed specifically for multi-label tasks. 

The HCP framework operates by taking an arbitrary number of object segment hypotheses as input. These hypotheses are processed through a shared CNN, and their output scores are subsequently aggregated via a cross-hypothesis max-pooling layer to generate final multi-label predictions. Key advantages of this architecture include: 1) it eliminates the need for ground-truth bounding box annotations during training; 2) the max-pooling mechanism ensures robustness against noisy or redundant hypotheses; 3) the shared CNN can be effectively pre-trained on massive single-label datasets like ImageNet to compensate for limited multi-label data; and 4) the system naturally produces multi-label outputs without requiring explicit labels for each individual hypothesis. 

Extensive evaluations on the Pascal VOC 2007 and 2012 benchmarks demonstrate that HCP significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. Notably, on the VOC 2012 dataset, HCP achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 84.2% on its own, which rises to a record-breaking 90.3% when integrated with complementary hand-crafted features.
